speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of an interview with Dr. Vera Laska. Dr. Laska, you were describing what happened to you when you arrived at Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were assigned to these two factories, and I was assigned to a machine which was, oh, about five meters long. And it had big spools-- what is that, about a foot? And behind my machine-- oh, the-- this five-meter long machine had I don't know how many dozens of these big spools-- and they were fed from behind my machine from big bins. There were strips of cotton about-- what is that, four inches, five inches wide-- and half an inch thick. And they went through a-- a row of needles, and evidently those needles divided that-- that strip of cotton. And so then, by the time I saw the cotton, it came in a very thick thread, I would say, about like my finger. And the spools were winding it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had on top of these spools-- there were two rows of spools-- and then there was a-- an iron rod. And the iron rod I could stop or start. All the-- all the spools started by that rod. And of course, whenever the supervisor went to the john or looked out the window, we would stop this thing, [LAUGHS] just watching the supervisor-- German supervisor, a civilian. And when she start returning, we started the machine, to slow down the production, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or we learned very well how to, just with a touch of a finger, we could break the thread. And of course the moment the thread was broken, we had to stop the machine and reattach the thread. That took a little time. So that was the little thing we could do, you know, for the Allies, to-- to slow down the War Production. But multiply that with the hundreds of thousands of prisoners. So it did amount to something, I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, it-- it was relatively-- underlined, relatively-- very good. Nothing like Auschwitz. First of all, we were sort of in the woods. It was a village, but we were-- and the factory was in the village-- but we were housed, I don't know, maybe a kilometer outside the village. And we could see woods. And once in a blue moon, we could see a wildflower. And we had one little barrack which had, I don't know, maybe six faucets. There were about 500 of us only, or maybe 600 or so, in several barracks. And there was one barrack for the German supervisors, and a doctor. And the doctor was a Russian prisoner. And if she was a doctor, I'm Maria Theresa. Maybe, maybe she was a nurse, because she didn't know from Adam. There was, with us, a woman whose husband was a doctor, and she had some conversations with this Russian doctor-- or Ukrainian, I forget. They were all Russian to us. And she said, if she's a doctor, education must be strange in Russia, in the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow. And-- so that's where the clock was, in that special barrack. But we could at least wash our-- no hot water, but water! You know? And we were divided into separate barracks, and there were only two layers, not-- not three.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the food was relatively better. I think we got something for breakfast beside the bread came at breakfast. And sometimes we got a piece of cheese or a piece of sausage. And then for lunch was the usually the soup that they brought to the factory. We gobbled it down quickly. And what was very nice, we walked to work and from work, which gave us some exercise and, sort of, we could look. But we were with SS guards, nevertheless. But at least we could see color, and once in awhile, we see-- could see people on the street. And the factory had some German civilians, like the old Meister, who must have been 70. He was the top boss. He was sort of a-- a good guy. I think that he didn't agree with all this prisoner business that he had to supervise. Not that he wasn't a, you know, a Nazi, but I don't think he agreed that people should be forced to do things. Then there was the young master, who was a son of a bitch, but he was lame and that's why he was at home. And we had to watch out for him. When he came around, everybody was working like mad. And then were local women, also, additional workers. But everybody who wasn't us was a supervisor, you know, we had to watch out for.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had three layers of people above us, and in the door, always, stood the SS, you know, with either a holster, a gun, or whatever. Watching. And where they got these SS women, I don't know. We knew that some of them were maids before, or washer women. And yet, they put the uniform on them, and they had the power of life and death over thousands of people. The could shoot you and nothing happened to them in the line of duty. So, Gross Rosen was OK in this subdivision. Funny, I wanted to go back there, too, and we couldn't find the village. And I knew the name of the village. They changed the name of the village. I don't remember what. It may be somewhere written down from my trip, but I didn't look at my notes yet. What was the name of the village when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was [INAUDIBLE], but there is no such village nowadays. And finally, we found an old-- we were giving up. We were in the village. I didn't recognize the village, of course. Trees grow, buildings go down, buildings go up. Things look different. It's hard to believe. I hardly recognized the villa where I grew up with my grandparents-- and it was a very special, big, huge villa-- because they changed the color, the shape, they added, they took-- So finally we found an old timer on our drive out of the village. He was just sitting on the fence, so we stopped the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember-- oh, that is right out here. And that factory? You're looking at it. So we went back, and there was a guard. By this time, it was about 4:00 in the afternoon. We were looking from 10:00 a.m. And the place looked like buttoned up. The guard in the window-- this is still-- no, this is after Communism. This was after East Germany was free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet, he still somehow behaved communistically. Wouldn't let us in. And we explained, and I gave him some dollars, and because of the couple of dollars, well, I'll let you into the courtyard, but there's a guard. And he called somebody who was a fire guard, some young man. And the fire guard took us to this big gate and into the yard, and I could look up and I think I recognized the window. But he wouldn't let us in. We might have bombed the place or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were-- at this point, who were the other prisoners that you were with? Were they all political prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, yeah. Either that, or it's a mixed bag. You know, you have professional people, a couple of prostitutes thrown in, whoever was among the-- among the transport. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they from many different countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: From several, yeah. We had French. We had Poles. Poles everywhere were prevalent, because numerically, most of the victims among the Jews and most of the prisoners were Polish. Poland was big. You know, I don't know how many millions. And the same is true about the next place where, again, I had this plush job of measuring that little ring that I was talking-- talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Gross Rosen from about-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, from July of '44 till briefly after the new year of '45, because I remember Christmas and New Year's there. And shortly thereafter, we were moved-- evacuated. And the reason, of course, why the-- the Germans bothered, by that stage of the game, to evacuate us, because if they ran away because they're afraid of the Russian front approaching, they would be able-- as long as they herded us in front of them, they were doing their duty. Saving the labor force.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were the beneficiary. Instead of being shot or abandoned or whatever, we were herded toward the center of Germany. So we ended up-- I mean, my transport eventually ended up being shifted to central Germany. We were in Nordhausen for several months, and then we went on the hunger march. We didn't know, toward Mauthausen, but I didn't wait until we got there. And where were you taken after Grossroseln?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Grossroseln, Nordhausen. The city is Nordhausen, but we were not in Nordhausen itself. We were in a village next to Nordhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that I knew because we were looking for it. I had a very good atlas before we went on that trip. Gross-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Grosswerd-- I think it was Grosswerder, or very close to it. There was a Kleinwerder, Grosswerder, or Kleinhunder, Grosshunder. It's on any detailed map. And we were in Gross-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not [? -munder. ?] It's very similar. If I would pick up the atlas, I would find it. What a shame that I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you there? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: There, we worked in the tunnel, in the tunnel which belonged to Camp Dora. Huge camp. Again, I don't know, I forget, how many subdivisions. And there were lots of French and Belgians there, males. They had 10,000 French prisoners of war in barracks on top of the tunnel, and freight trains went into the tunnel. It was so big. To the best of my knowledge, two floors. Maybe somebody claims it was three. I don't remember. I seem to recall two floors, and huge, huge-- entire factory in the tunnel. And to the-- one thing that they were doing there was parts, or they put together, among others, those buzz bombs, that new German invention of-- what is it-- flying rockets or whatever they are. Frau eins, or Frau zwei. And Frau zwei. And there my job was why I called it plush job. I could stand or I could sit, even, and measure, with a micrometer, which is a little gadget, you measure the thickness of a little metal ring. Diameter was the word that I was looking for. Twice the radius is a diameter. OK. So I was measuring the diameter and the thickness of the ring, and all the good ones were supposed to go, I don't know, to this bin, and the bad ones to the other bin. So I was very just and divided them 50-50. One went here, one went there. And Mr. Churchill said, after the war, that ever since they put political prisoners in the tunnel, the buzz bombs didn't work so well. Many fell down and didn't explode. So I take a millionth of a millionth percentage credit for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actively doing something to sabotage the bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm telling you. I just pretended to measure. One went here, one went there. Good, bad, I couldn't care less. Oh, I did care. You know, I didn't want to measure and put the good ones and bad ones there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that an organized effort with other prisoners or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, most people. Slowdown was an instinctive thing. Whenever you watched a supervisor turn his back or-- or look out the window or go to the john, whatever, you immediately slowed down. First of all, you saved energy. Self-preservation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And second, the hell with them. They want me to do this? I won't do it. Or I slow down. The moment they came back, you had to work, or else, you know? Because they could take your food away. They could beat you. About how old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: How what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point, that you were working in the tunnel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you add it up. I must have been about, what, 17 or so?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your living conditions like at Dora Nordhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Nordhausen, this must have been the really desperate times by now, the Germans. Because the village wasn't set up for prisoners at all. These French prisoners of war were in barracks on top of the tunnel, so that was part of whoever did the tunnel. And then they got these new-- I don't know why they got us there. They needed extra work. This is about February of '45. January or February. We were housed in a house which must have been something like a community center, because we were bedded down on the floor of something that-- like a theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a stage, actually, with a curtain, you know? So, that's how we were bedded down, on the floor one next to another, like two of us on that rug, you know? Mattress on mattresses. Did have straw. We did have a blanket. And the-- the food was mostly that so-called soup with potatoes or-- it wasn't much better than Gross Rosen, because this was really the tail end of the war by this time. The Germans knew, chances are nil that they would win the war, you know? And the sanitary conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to stand in line for the john. There were maybe couple of johns. [SIGHS] I don't remember. I don't [?see that?]. I know that somewhere, for the first time, I saw johns in a strange shape-- I forget where it was, whether it was right here-- that I later saw in the Orient. The bowls had a completely different shape. But I don't see. I see the stage. I see a little tiny room, not bigger than maybe twice my couch-- three times my couch-- which must have been a dressing room, which a friend of mine and I sort of declared, that's our room. That was it. Nobody discovered it. We discovered it, and we took our mattresses there, and I remember getting hold of some scraps of newspaper with a picture of a horse. I don't particularly like horses. Somehow put the picture of a horse on the wall. That was art. That was a sign of another life. But we didn't enjoy our room too long, because then we were taken on that march, the hunger march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with the original four members of your-- other four members of the Auschwitz group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we still were together until the hunger march, at which point, I told [? Veritza, ?] the one who's in Yugoslavia-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's so hard to believe that [? Veritza ?] is more than 60 years old. Anyhow. And I said, watch me. I am going to lots of good confusion, because it was such a mess that you wouldn't believe this. German refugees. Army refugees. Our refugees. Jewish refugees. You name it. People going with pieces of furniture on top of a horse one way, and others went-- it was unbelievable on the highways. Military machinery, and we all had to go into the ditch because the military had to go through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, lot of confusion, I'm going to try to escape. And I wasn't the first one, you know. And I said, if you want to run with me, don't move from my side. Because if the chance comes, I go. You are there, you're not there, I go. Don't move from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she did. Because we got to this village, and there was a cauldron, but cauldron, you know, like they make plum preserves. Oh, you wouldn't know about plum preserves in Europe. Anyhow, a big cauldron of boiled potatoes. And not for us. For the German refugees who were coming through, running from the Russians. And our group who just zoom on that cauldron of boiled potatoes, including [? Veritza. ?] And I went in the other direction, over the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, of course, we met and she said she was crying for two days. I said, well, you were stupid. I told you, stick to me every moment. But the potatoes! I said, so you had potatoes, I had freedom. So what? Anyhow. [LAUGHS] She's-- well, so that's how I liberated myself. When was it that the death march began, and what were you told by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Told? Told nothing. Told, get off your ass and march. They didn't announce, we are going here or we are going there. We are just going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: So this must have been March 1945, but what day of March, I don't know. What a shame that I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you march before you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched at least three nights-- or three days and three nights-- and meanwhile, you know, they tried to bed us down in all kinds of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they call those big houses for the hay? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, come on. I usually am not at loss for words. Where do you keep the horses and the cows and the hay?

speaker: interviewer
text: Barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barn. Barn. Huge barns. And we were lucky because they might have some straw. We slept helter skelter, you know, and once we-- we just stayed outside, and it was bitter cold. Bitter cold. Many people died on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with, at that point, just political prisoners or all kinds of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, we were so mixed that-- and we didn't ask, who are you? [PAUSES FOR  3 SECONDS] And out of Auschwitz, we no longer had triangles. They didn't want us to wear triangles because we were mingling with Germans in the factories, you know? So it was-- it was a mixed bag. And Poles, Poles all over the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the SS guards treat you on that march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roughly. And, of course, the-- the worst treatment came to those who couldn't march anymore, and they would sit down and the crowd would continue. And those who stayed behind were shot. We heard the shots. We heard the shots, and we saw-- you know, these lines were so long. They must have been miles and miles. And I was surprised that they shot people in view of the German population. And there were people to the right, to the left. Dead horses, dead people, and you didn't know whether they are German refugees, or whether they are Jews, or whether they are just one of us. They wouldn't be one of us because we were marching on, and we would leave a few behind, and then you heard the shots. And no food. And you can barely walk. Miserable shoes. Most shoes were too big or too small.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Some kind-- I'm sure it wasn't those damn black shoes anymore. I have no idea. But I had something on my feet, and I had two shoes that matched, because many people had one right shoe, one left shoe that didn't match, which is horrible. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS] no stockings, and that is something that's very rough on your feet if you don't have something between your skin and-- and the shoe. At least, to me it's very rough. And so, whatever-- if we could get hold of some rag, or even paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, to get a piece of paper was not like, get a piece of paper. Where? From where? Not even in the johns did we find newspapers, because that was what the Germans used at that time. They cut squares like that from newspaper, and not even that was on the john, so that we wouldn't find any news from the newspaper. So when we found a little piece of paper, put it in the shoe quickly. But how long does that last? Some people had stockings, organized somewhere. Do you remember the reaction of people as you were marching by, of some of the townspeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my dear. Everybody had their own trouble. Sometimes they would say, where are you coming from? Who are you? Once in a blue moon. Or we asked. But most of us-- I- I remember marching, just keeping my mouth shut because even to open your mouth took energy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Plus, we probably didn't give a damn. But when we saw German refugees, I remember a feeling of joy. So that's what, finally, you're getting what's coming to you. Because all Germans we stuck into the same bank at that stage of the game. That's a normal human reaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening with the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that they are getting a tremendous beating from the Russians, because we were going from the east toward the west. But to tell you how without news we were, the invasion was June 6, 1944. Of course, we knew, quote unquote, by May, there was an invasion. But it was wishful thinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we found out for sure, that reliably, that there was-- there had been an invasion in June in October. That we really knew, not just rumors, you know? There was lots of latrine rumors. Latrine was the cultural center everywhere. Can you describe what happened to you when you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I fell into a pile of manure, and I remembered some old saying that manure, if you step into it, it's good luck. Did you hear that? No? That is all over Europe. They say, oh, you stepped into dog doo. That's good luck. So I said, good luck. Well, I got myself out of there and ran while everybody was around that potato cauldron. And there was one big building and I ran into that big building. It had a huge open door and it looked like a barn. It was a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was one of those double barns, you know? There was something on the right side, something on the left side, and an empty sort of corridor in the middle. And I ran. And there was a-- a ladder standing there, and I ran up that ladder and there was lots of straw. And why, I will never know, I did one of the smartest things in my life, I think. As I was up there, I took the ladder and moved it, and it fell against the other side of the barn. You understand? Like this. Here was the ladder. Here was the little empty space. Here was the other part of-- and I moved the ladder and it went this way. OK. And I stayed there, quiet as a mouse. And I heard some voices in German. I mean, in good German, not lager German, saying, 'Sonia, search! Search, Sonia!' And I thought, Sonia. It was a woman's name, Sonia. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a dog's name, was Sonia, and they wanted a dog. And the dog came and I'm lost. And the dog was barking upwards, so the peasant or the farmer went up that ladder, looking, and there was nobody there. I was on the other side, straw-- straw all over me, you know, quiet. And that was it. And I stayed there and nobody came to that barn for four and a half days.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you there for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
